title: NIS2 Cryptography and Encryption Policy Monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(h) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) (Cryptography and encryption) is enforced â€” Splunk UC-22.2.11: NIS2 Cryptography and Encryption Policy Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.11' running on CIM Certificate data model, index=network (TLS metadata), index=vulnerability (crypto-related findings), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted â€” SME review required.

dataSources: Accelerated Splunk CIM Certificates data model (`datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates`, fields ssl_subject_common_name, ssl_issuer_common_name, ssl_end_time); `index=pki` `sourcetype=stream:tls` from Splunk Add-on for Stream ([Splunkbase 1809](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1809)); `index=certificates` `sourcetype=certificate:inventory` for CMDB scans; optional Tenable NVS feed `index=vulnerability`.
app: Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates
  where All_Certificates.ssl_end_time < relative_time(now(), "+30d")
  by All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name All_Certificates.ssl_end_time All_Certificates.ssl_issuer_common_name
| rename All_Certificates.* as *
| eval days_until_expiry=round((ssl_end_time - now())/86400, 0)
| eval status=case(days_until_expiry < 0, "EXPIRED", days_until_expiry < 14, "CRITICAL", days_until_expiry < 30, "WARNING")
| sort days_until_expiry
| table ssl_subject_common_name, ssl_issuer_common_name, days_until_expiry, status
description: Surfaces X.509 certificates whose `ssl_end_time` falls inside a thirty-day horizon using accelerated CIM Certificate summaries so crypto governance teams can renew before services break or downgrade ciphers.
value: Expired orsoon-to-expire keys silently undermine encrypted channels that regulators expect to stay trustworthy; anchoring renewals to Splunk schedules gives measurable lead time, owner accountability, and historical proof that the organization actively managed cryptographyâ€”not just bought it once.
implementation: (1) Install CIM ([Splunkbase 1621](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1621)) and accelerate **Certificates**. (2) Forward Stream `stream:tls` to `index=pki`. (3) Import inventory scanner output as `certificate:inventory`. (4) Map Tenable plugin families about weak crypto to `index=vulnerability`. (5) Maintain `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv` with compensating controls. (6) Email PKI ops when `status=CRITICAL`.
visualization: Table of expiring subjects, pie chart of issuer share, overlay of renewal tickets from ServiceNow.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t latest(Certificates.x509_ssl_hash) from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by Certificates.ssl_subject | head 10000
knownFalsePositives: 1) **Short-lived Let's Encrypt auto-renewals** tripping WARN every sixty days. *Distinguish:* issuer `ssl_issuer_common_name` contains "Let's Encrypt". *Suppress:* `where not match(ssl_issuer_common_name,"(?i)let")`. 2) **Tenable reporting duplicate certs per IP** when SNI not captured. *Distinguish:* same `ssl_subject_common_name` with different `dest_ip`. *Suppress:* `| stats min(days_until_expiry) as days_until_expiry by ssl_subject_common_name`. 3) **Stream sampling misses occasional handshakes** so `status` flips stale. *Distinguish:* `index=certificates` inventory shows newer `ssl_end_time`. *Suppress:* prefer inventory lookup when `_time` within 24h. 4) **Internal CA names flagged weak by generic regex** because of legacy templates still permitted under national banking guidance. *Distinguish:* serial listed in `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv`. *Suppress:* `lookup nis2_crypto_exception_register ssl_subject OUTPUT waive | where isnull(waive)`. 5) **Dev/test clusters inheriting wildcard prod certs** creating noise. *Distinguish:* host suffix `.dev.`. *Suppress:* filter `where NOT match(ssl_subject_common_name,"(?i)\\*.dev")`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
â€˘ Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809) â‰Ą8.0 deployed as an Independent Stream Forwarder (ISF) on a dedicated capture host. The ISF performs passive packet capture on a mirror/SPAN port â€” it does NOT intercept or modify traffic. The capture host must have a network interface connected to a SPAN port or TAP that mirrors the production VLANs carrying TLS traffic. Sizing: a 10 Gbps mirror port with 30% utilisation produces ~3 Gbps of traffic; at metadata-only capture (no payload), Stream generates ~5-15 MB/min of TLS handshake metadata. The ISF needs 4 CPU cores, 8 GB RAM, and 100 GB local disk for buffering.
â€˘ Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621) â‰Ą5.3 installed on Search Heads and Indexers. This provides the Certificates data model (`Certificates.All_Certificates`) with fields: `ssl_subject_common_name`, `ssl_issuer_common_name`, `ssl_end_time` (epoch), `ssl_start_time`, `ssl_serial`, `ssl_publickey_algorithm`, `ssl_signature_algorithm`, `ssl_version`. The data model normalises certificate data from multiple sourcetypes (Stream TLS, inventory scanners, vulnerability scanners) into a single queryable schema.
â€˘ Data model acceleration: enable acceleration on the Certificates data model via Settings > Data Models > Certificates > Edit > Acceleration. Set summary range to at least 90 days (covers a full certificate renewal cycle for most enterprise CAs). Storage estimate: ~500 MB per 10,000 unique certificates over 90 days. The accelerated `| tstats` query is 10-50x faster than raw event searches on large PKI deployments.
â€˘ Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) â‰Ą7.0 optional but recommended for asset framework integration. ES provides `assets_by_str` lookups that map `ssl_subject_common_name` to a business owner, enabling automated ticket routing to the correct PKI team. Without ES, you need to maintain a manual `nis2_pki_owner.csv` lookup.
â€˘ Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060) optional for vulnerability-scan-derived certificate data. Tenable plugin families 33929 (SSL Certificate Expiry), 57582 (SSL Self-Signed Certificate), 42263 (SSL Certificate with Wrong Hostname), and 20007 (SSL Version Detection) provide complementary certificate findings that can be correlated with Stream-observed certificates.
â€˘ Index `pki` for Stream TLS metadata with retention â‰Ą 90 days. Index `certificates` for certificate inventory scans. Index `vulnerability` for Tenable findings. Index `audit_evidence` for compliance evidence output (retention â‰Ą 7 years).
â€˘ RBAC: create role `pki_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = pki, certificates, vulnerability, audit_evidence`. Certificate metadata exposes internal hostnames, CA hierarchies, and key algorithms â€” restrict access.
â€˘ Baseline knowledge: X.509 certificate lifecycle â€” certificates have a `notBefore` (start) and `notAfter` (expiry) date. When a certificate expires, TLS connections to that service fail with browser/client errors. NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) requires entities to have policies on cryptography and encryption; this UC monitors the operational health of those policies by tracking certificate expiry and protocol version compliance.

Step 1 â€” Configure data collection
**Stream TLS metadata capture:**
On the ISF, configure TLS protocol capture via the Stream web UI (Configure > Protocols > TLS) or by editing `/opt/streamfwd/local/streamfwd.conf`:

- **Capture mode**: Metadata only (no payload) â€” this is critical for GDPR compliance. Stream extracts the TLS handshake (ClientHello, ServerHello, Certificate exchange) but does not capture encrypted application data. The metadata includes: `tls_version`, `cipher_suite`, server certificate chain (subject, issuer, expiry, serial, key algorithm, signature algorithm), `server_name` (SNI), and handshake timing.
- **Port filter**: `443, 8443, 6443, 993, 995, 465, 636, 5986` â€” cover HTTPS, Kubernetes API, IMAPS, POP3S, SMTPS, LDAPS, WinRM-TLS. Add custom ports as needed for internal services.
- **Index**: `pki`
- **Sourcetype**: `stream:tls` (ISF default â€” do not override)

Verification after the first capture cycle (~2 minutes):
```spl
index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-10m
| stats dc(ssl_subject_common_name) as unique_certs, dc(dest_ip) as unique_servers, dc(tls_version) as tls_versions
```
`unique_certs` should be > 0 â€” if zero, the mirror port is not forwarding TLS traffic to the ISF, or the ISF's capture interface is on the wrong VLAN. `unique_servers` should roughly match the number of TLS-enabled servers visible from the mirrored segment.

**Certificate inventory scanning:**
For comprehensive coverage, supplement Stream (which only sees certificates from observed connections) with a nightly certificate inventory scan. Use a scanner tool (e.g., `ssl-cert-check`, `certigo`, or a custom script using `openssl s_client`) that connects to each service endpoint and extracts the certificate chain. Write results to HEC as `sourcetype=certificate:inventory` with fields: `ssl_subject_common_name`, `ssl_issuer_common_name`, `ssl_end_time` (epoch), `ssl_serial`, `ssl_publickey_algorithm`, `ssl_key_length`, `ssl_signature_algorithm`, `dest_ip`, `dest_port`.

Example scanner script (runs nightly via cron):
```bash
#!/bin/bash
while IFS=, read -r ip port service; do
  expiry=$(echo | openssl s_client -connect "$ip:$port" -servername "$service" 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -enddate 2>/dev/null | cut -d= -f2)
  epoch=$(date -d "$expiry" +%s 2>/dev/null)
  curl -s -k "https://<HF>:8088/services/collector/event" \
    -H "Authorization: Splunk <TOKEN>" \
    -d "{\"event\":{\"ssl_subject_common_name\":\"$service\",\"ssl_end_time\":$epoch,\"dest_ip\":\"$ip\",\"dest_port\":\"$port\"}}"
done < /opt/scripts/tls_endpoints.csv
```

**Tenable integration (optional):**
Configure the Tenable Add-On for Splunk to poll Tenable.io vulnerability exports. Filter to plugin families related to cryptography: 33929 (expiry), 57582 (self-signed), 42263 (hostname mismatch), 20007 (SSL version), 51192 (weak ciphers). These arrive as `index=vulnerability sourcetype=tenable:sc:vuln` or `sourcetype=tenable:io:vuln`.

Step 2 â€” Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.11 NIS2 Certificate Expiry Monitor`.

Primary search (CIM-accelerated):
```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count
  from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates
  where All_Certificates.ssl_end_time < relative_time(now(), "+30d")
  by All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name All_Certificates.ssl_end_time All_Certificates.ssl_issuer_common_name
| rename All_Certificates.* as *
| eval days_until_expiry=round((ssl_end_time - now())/86400, 0)
| eval status=case(days_until_expiry < 0, "EXPIRED", days_until_expiry < 14, "CRITICAL", days_until_expiry < 30, "WARNING")
| sort days_until_expiry
| table ssl_subject_common_name, ssl_issuer_common_name, days_until_expiry, status
```

Understanding this SPL â€” design decisions:

â€˘ **`| tstats summariesonly=t`**: queries the pre-accelerated Certificates data model summary instead of raw events. This is 10-50x faster than `search index=pki` because the data model pre-computes field values and stores them in TSIDX files. The `summariesonly=t` flag ensures only accelerated data is queried â€” if the DMA is behind, recent certificates may be missed (see Troubleshooting).

â€˘ **`ssl_end_time < relative_time(now(), "+30d")`**: selects certificates expiring within 30 days. The 30-day horizon gives the PKI team enough lead time to renew. For organisations with slower change processes, extend to 60 or 90 days.

â€˘ **Three-tier status classification**: `EXPIRED` (already past expiry â€” immediate action needed), `CRITICAL` (< 14 days â€” renewal must be in progress), `WARNING` (< 30 days â€” plan renewal). These map to different runbook actions and notification urgencies.

â€˘ **Why `ssl_subject_common_name` not `dest_ip`**: a single certificate (wildcard like `*.example.com`) may be observed on many IPs. Grouping by subject de-duplicates â€” the PKI team renews certificates, not IPs.

Companion search for weak TLS protocols (save as `UC-22.2.11-weak-tls`):
```spl
index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-24h tls_version=*
| eval weak=if(match(tls_version, "SSLv|TLSv1\.0|TLSv1\.1"), 1, 0)
| where weak=1
| stats count as sessions dc(ssl_subject_common_name) as unique_certs by dest_ip tls_version cipher_suite
| sort - sessions
| table dest_ip, tls_version, cipher_suite, sessions, unique_certs
```

This surfaces servers still negotiating TLS 1.0/1.1 or SSLv3 â€” protocols with known vulnerabilities (POODLE, BEAST). NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) implicitly requires modern cryptography; TLS 1.2+ is the minimum acceptable version per ENISA guidance.

Schedule (certificate expiry): cron `0 7 * * *` (daily at 07:00). Trigger: `Number of results > 0`. Throttle by `ssl_subject_common_name` for 24 hours.
Schedule (weak TLS): cron `0 8 * * 1` (weekly Monday 08:00). Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) For `status=CRITICAL` or `status=EXPIRED`: email PKI operations team with the certificate subject, issuer, and days until expiry. Create a ServiceNow incident via alert action. (2) For `status=WARNING`: email only (no ticket). (3) For weak TLS: email security engineering team with the server IP and observed TLS version. (4) Write all results to `index=audit_evidence`.

Step 3 â€” Validate
(a) **Certificate expiry parity check**: pick a known certificate (e.g., the main website's cert). In Splunk: `| tstats summariesonly=t latest(All_Certificates.ssl_end_time) as expiry from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates where All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name="www.example.com" | eval human=strftime(expiry, "%Y-%m-%d")`. From the command line: `echo | openssl s_client -connect www.example.com:443 -servername www.example.com 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -enddate`. Both should show the same expiry date (Â±1 day for timezone differences).

(b) **TLS version parity check**: pick a server from the weak-TLS results. Verify with `openssl s_client -connect <dest_ip>:<dest_port> -tls1` (tests TLS 1.0). If the connection succeeds, the server genuinely supports TLS 1.0 â€” the Stream observation is correct. If it fails, Stream may be capturing a TLS downgrade from a middle-box (see Troubleshooting).

(c) **Data model acceleration health**: `| rest /services/admin/summarization by_tstats=t | search datamodel_name=Certificates | table datamodel_name eai:acl.app is_inprogress latest_time complete_pct`. `complete_pct` should be > 95%. If `is_inprogress=1` and `latest_time` is more than 1 hour behind, acceleration is lagging.

(d) **Count parity**: `| tstats summariesonly=t dc(All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name) as dm_count from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates` vs `index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-7d | stats dc(ssl_subject_common_name) as raw_count`. If `dm_count` is significantly less than `raw_count`, the CIM event types/tags are not mapping Stream events to the Certificates data model â€” check `eventtypes.conf` and `tags.conf` in the CIM Add-on.

(e) **Let's Encrypt auto-renewal test**: if you use Let's Encrypt certificates (90-day lifetime, auto-renewed at 60 days), confirm they appear in the 30-day window during the renewal period. They should trigger `WARNING` status and then disappear once the new cert is issued. If they persist as `CRITICAL` after renewal, Stream is caching the old certificate observation â€” wait for the next TLS handshake to be captured.

Step 4 â€” Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Cryptography Posture`):
â€˘ **Row 1 â€” Headline tiles**: Single-value `Expired certificates` (red â‰Ą 1). Single-value `Certificates expiring < 14 days` (amber â‰Ą 1). Single-value `Servers using TLS 1.0/1.1` (red â‰Ą 1). Single-value `Total monitored certificates` (informational).
â€˘ **Row 2 â€” Certificate renewal table**: sortable by ssl_subject_common_name, ssl_issuer_common_name, days_until_expiry, status. Color-coded rows: red = EXPIRED, amber = CRITICAL, yellow = WARNING. Drilldown opens the certificate's complete detail (key algorithm, signature algorithm, SAN list) from `index=pki` or `index=certificates`.
â€˘ **Row 3 â€” Issuer distribution**: pie chart of `ssl_issuer_common_name`. Shows the CA mix â€” useful for identifying single-CA concentration risk (if one CA is compromised, what percentage of your certificates are affected?).
â€˘ **Row 4 â€” Weak TLS servers**: table of dest_ip, tls_version, cipher_suite, sessions. Drilldown shows the full TLS negotiation details from Stream.
â€˘ **Row 5 â€” 90-day renewal trend**: timechart from `index=audit_evidence` showing certificate-expiry finding count over 90 days. Declining trend = PKI team is catching up. Rising trend = certificate sprawl or renewal process gaps.

Runbook (owner: PKI Operations):
1. Daily 07:15: review the dashboard.
2. `EXPIRED` certificates: immediate action â€” renew or replace. Check if the service is still running (it may be failing silently with TLS errors that users are ignoring or bypassing). Verify with `curl -v https://<service>` and check for certificate errors.
3. `CRITICAL` (<14 days): confirm a renewal ticket exists in ServiceNow. If not, create one with `urgency=high`. Contact the certificate owner (from ES asset lookup or `nis2_pki_owner.csv`).
4. `WARNING` (<30 days): informational â€” no immediate action, but the PKI team should plan the renewal. Auto-renewal systems (ACME/Let's Encrypt, cert-manager in Kubernetes) should handle these automatically.
5. Weak TLS servers: create a remediation ticket for the server owner. Include the `tls_version` observed and the target version (TLS 1.2 minimum, TLS 1.3 preferred). Some legacy systems cannot upgrade â€” document exceptions in `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv` with compensating controls (e.g., network segmentation, WAF fronting).
6. Monthly: export the dashboard as PDF, sign with RFC 3161 TSA, archive to `index=audit_evidence` as the Art.21(2)(h) compliance artifact.

Step 5 â€” Troubleshooting

â€˘ **`| tstats` returns zero rows.** The Certificates data model is not accelerated, or no events are mapped to it. Fix: (a) Check acceleration: Settings > Data Models > Certificates > Edit > Acceleration â€” enable and set range to 90 days. (b) Check event mapping: `index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-1h | head 1 | table eventtype tag::eventtype`. The events must be tagged with `eventtype=stream_tls_cert` and `tag=certificate` (or equivalent CIM tags). If tags are missing, the CIM Add-on or Stream TA is not installed on the Search Head.

â€˘ **Stream sees only TLS 1.0 connections even though servers support TLS 1.3.** The mirror/SPAN port is configured on a network segment where a TLS-terminating load balancer (F5, HAProxy, Nginx) re-encrypts with a weaker cipher to the backend. Stream sees the backend-to-server TLS, not the client-to-LB TLS. Fix: mirror the client-facing segment instead, or accept that the backend segment uses internal TLS standards (document the exception).

â€˘ **Duplicate `ssl_subject_common_name` entries from multi-region DCs.** The same wildcard certificate (e.g., `*.example.com`) is observed on 50 different IPs across data centres. This inflates the count. Fix: the SPL already groups by `ssl_subject_common_name` which de-duplicates. If you also want to see the IP distribution: `| stats min(days_until_expiry) as days dc(dest_ip) as server_count by ssl_subject_common_name | sort days`.

â€˘ **`ssl_end_time` shows dates in 1970 (epoch = 0).** The Stream TA could not parse the certificate's `notAfter` field. Common cause: the certificate uses a non-standard date format, or the certificate is a self-signed cert with no valid expiry. Fix: check `index=pki sourcetype=stream:tls ssl_end_time=0 earliest=-1h | head 5 | table _raw` to see the raw certificate data.

â€˘ **False EXPIRED status from host clock skew.** The forwarder's clock is ahead of the Splunk indexer, causing `now()` in the SPL to be ahead of the actual time. A certificate that is not yet expired appears expired. Fix: verify NTP on the Search Head (`chronyc sources` or `ntpq -p`). The SPL runs on the SH, so the SH clock is the one that matters for `now()`.

â€˘ **Alert fires every 85 days for Let's Encrypt certificates.** Let's Encrypt issues 90-day certificates and auto-renews at 60 days. Between day 60 and renewal completion (typically < 1 hour), the old certificate briefly appears in the 30-day window. Fix: either suppress Let's Encrypt certificates (`where NOT match(ssl_issuer_common_name, "(?i)let.s.encrypt")`) or accept these as informational alerts that self-clear within hours.

â€˘ **Stream captures certificates from internal services that are not NIS2-relevant.** The mirror port sees all TLS traffic, including internal tooling, dev environments, and monitoring systems. The search produces hundreds of findings for non-critical services. Fix: add a scoping lookup `nis2_crypto_scope.csv` with `ssl_subject_common_name, in_scope` and filter: `| lookup nis2_crypto_scope.csv ssl_subject_common_name OUTPUT in_scope | where in_scope="true"`. Alternatively, scope by `dest_ip` using the ES `assets_by_cidr` framework.

â€˘ **Tenable plugin 33929 reports different expiry than Stream.** Tenable scans at a point in time; Stream observes continuously. If a certificate was renewed between the Tenable scan and the Stream observation, they will disagree. Fix: always use the most recent observation: `| stats latest(ssl_end_time) as ssl_end_time by ssl_subject_common_name` across both sourcetypes.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on the electronic locks (certificates) that make websites and internal tools talk privately. When a lock is about to expire or is too old-fashioned, we get a heads-up so nothing suddenly stops working.
exclusions: Does not approve new PKI hierarchies, perform manual certificate rotations, waive regulatory mandates, or assess nation-state crypto regulations beyond organizational policy mapping.
evidence: Saved search outputs `UC-22.2.11` and `UC-22.2.11-stream`, supporting `stream:tls` samples for at least one incident review quarter, and TSA-sealed CSV archives per `evidenceSigning` retained â‰Ą7 years.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Lab forwards a synthesized `Certificates` summary whose `ssl_end_time` is ten days out for subject `wallet-api.local` while Stream shows `tls_version=TLSv1.0`; combined dashboard lights CRITICAL on renewal and weak protocol within the same five-minute test window.
controlTest.negativeScenario: All in-scope subjects carry `notAfter` beyond forty-five days, Stream reports nothing below TLSv1.2, and `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv` contains a valid waiver row for the lone legacy mainframe listener; nightly correlation search returns zero breaches after filters apply.

equipmentModels: Splunk Stream Independent Forwarder (wire data TLS metadata capture)
Tenable.io / Tenable.sc (vulnerability and certificate scan data)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (asset framework, notable routing)
Splunk Common Information Model Add-on 5.x (Certificates data model)
